lesson.menu_desc: When Things Go Wrong -- Managing Delays and Escalations -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 3 -- When Things Go Wrong -- Handling Delays and Escalations
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Fabiana Michelly Silva | Aula 3 -- When Things Go Wrong

listenings.text: Hi Fabiana, this is Klaus from TechFlow. I am calling with some difficult news about your APAC order. We have hit a serious bottleneck at our Munich plant, and a power outage caused several hours of downtime last week. As a result, there is now a significant backlog, and your shipment will face a delay of about ten days. I want to be transparent with you: we should have flagged the risk earlier, and we could have arranged a backup line. We are already working on a workaround through our Singapore facility, and I have put a contingency plan in place. I will send a full root cause report by Friday. Please call me back so we can discuss how to mitigate the impact. I am sorry for the disruption.

Hi team, here is a quick update on the TechFlow situation. As you know, a bottleneck at their Munich plant has caused a ten-day delay on our APAC shipment. I have reviewed the root cause report, and honestly, the supplier should have warned us sooner. If we had escalated this last month, we might have avoided the backlog entirely. For now, I have approved their workaround through Singapore as a contingency. To prevent a shortfall, I am asking the team to follow up with our backup vendor and prepare for possible downtime. We must hold the supplier accountable, but our priority right now is to mitigate the disruption and protect the next allocation. I will escalate to leadership if the setback grows. Let me know your thoughts.

extra_audio.text: Fabiana receives a voicemail about a delayed shipment. She reads the root cause report from the supplier. She escalates the issue to her manager. The team approves a workaround through Singapore. They put a contingency plan in place to prevent future setbacks.
